draft.introduction: Prudentius’s Contra Symmachum (c. 402 CE) is a verse refutation of Symmachus’s plea for the Altar of Victory. Prudentius argues that Rome’s greatness was providentially ordained to prepare the world for Christ, not earned by pagan sacrifice.

translation.text: Do you ask what road to take? Christ is the road. He himself said so, he pointed the way: “I am the Way.” He opened the path; follow the revealed road. Why do you wander through trackless wilds? The path lies open.
translation.original_text: Quaeris iter? Christus via est. Hoc ipse probavit, monstravitque viam: “Via sum” dixit. Ille viam patefecit: eas qua monstrat eundum. Quid per inaccessa erras? Patet area lata.

translator_notes.note: Prudentius directly answers Symmachus’s “one cannot arrive by one road alone” with Christ’s own claim from John 14:6. The exchange between Symmachus and his Christian opponents (Ambrose, Prudentius) is the defining literary confrontation between pagan pluralism and Christian exclusivism.
